content.content:
1

2 3

4 5

TeaParty

6 7

8 A research platform for durable, scalable agent coordination 9

10 11 > **Status: Experimental.** TeaParty is an active research project exploring how humans and AI agents can work together on complex tasks. The ideas are promising, the architecture is evolving, and things break regularly. Nothing here is production-ready. If you're interested in the research direction, welcome — but expect rough edges, incomplete features, and frequent redesigns. 12 13

14 Documentation · 15 Experimental Results · 16 Issues · 17 Contributing 18

19 20 --- 21 22 Current agent systems fail at four things: they treat requests as complete specifications (**intent gap**), they offer only a binary dial between full autonomy and constant oversight (**escalation failure**), they cannot recover when execution reveals that the plan — or the intent itself — was wrong (**no backtracking**), and they lose coherence as context grows (**context rot**). 23 24 TeaParty is building toward mixed teams where humans and AI agents collaborate on increasingly complex projects — not agents as tools, and not agents as autonomous replacements, but genuine teams where each member contributes what they do best. 25 26

27 28

29 30

The POC dashboard: projects, sessions with CfA state tracking, and task prompts at a glance.

31 32 ## Four Research Pillars 33 34 ### Conversation for Action 35 36 *Addresses: intent gap, backtracking* 37 38 A three-phase protocol — **Intent**, **Planning**, **Execution** — formalized as a state machine with explicit transitions and ten cross-phase backtracks. Each phase produces artifacts that make implicit context explicit. Approval gates between phases are learning opportunities where the system observes human corrections and preferences. 39 40 The key insight is backtracking. When execution reveals a flawed plan, the system returns to planning. When planning reveals a flawed intent, the system returns to intent. When final review reveals that the work faithfully implements an intent that turns out to be wrong, the system backtracks all the way from the end to the beginning. This is the most expensive transition — and without it, the only option is to ship work that misses the point. 41 42

43 44

45 46

The CfA state machine: three phases with synthesis loops and ten cross-phase backtrack transitions. Full specification →

47 48 ### Hierarchical Teams 49 50 *Addresses: context rot, scoping* 51 52 Complex work requires complex team structures. A single flat team trying to coordinate strategy and write every file hits context limits and loses coherence. TeaParty separates strategic coordination from tactical execution: an uber team manages strategy while subteams execute in parallel, each in its own process with its own context window. Liaison agents bridge levels, compressing context at each boundary. The uber lead never sees raw file content; subteam workers never see cross-team coordination. 53 54 ``` 55 Project Lead 56 / | \ 57 Coding Writing Research <- liaison agents (context boundary) 58 Liaison Liaison Liaison 59 | | | 60 [team] [team] [team] <- isolated subteams (separate processes) 61 ``` 62 63 [Hierarchical Teams →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/hierarchical-teams/) 64 65 ### Human Proxy Agents 66 67 *Addresses: escalation failure* 68 69 As agent teams grow, the human becomes a bottleneck. The proxy agent learns to stand in for the human — answering clarifying questions, responding to escalations, approving plans — based on an evolving model of what the human would decide. Asymmetric regret weighting ensures false approvals cost more than false escalations. The proxy earns autonomy through demonstrated alignment, not configuration. 70 71 The proxy's governing principle: **understand first, act second.** Before agents produce artifacts, the proxy runs an intake dialog — formulating predictions about what the human wants and checking them. Where predictions are confident, it answers for the human. Where they're uncertain, it escalates. The delta between predictions and actual answers is the highest-value learning signal in the system. 72 73 [Human Proxy Agents →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/human-proxies/) 74 75 ### Hierarchical Memory and Learning 76 77 *Addresses: context rot, scoping* 78 79 Learning is a retrieval problem — getting the right knowledge to the right agent at the right moment. Three learning purposes (organizational norms, task procedures, proxy preferences) feed a promotion chain that moves validated learnings up through the hierarchy. Four learning moments (prospective, in-flight, corrective, retrospective) capture knowledge at the points where it matters most. 80 81 [Learning System →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/learning-system/) 82 83 ## Bootstrapping 84 85 TeaParty eats its own dogfood. The platform's documentation, design artifacts, and implementation are produced by hierarchical agent teams running the TeaParty POC — the same coordination patterns we're researching are the ones we use to build. Every page in this documentation, every architectural decision, and every line of code has been produced or reviewed by agent teams operating under the protocols described above. 86 87 ## Quick Start 88 89 ### Prerequisites 90 91 - Python 3.11+ 92 - [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) package manager 93 - [Claude Code CLI](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) with a valid API key 94 95 ### Install 96 97 ```bash 98 git clone https://github.com/dlewissandy/teaparty.git 99 cd teaparty 100 uv sync 101 ``` 102 103 ### Run the HTML dashboard 104 105 ```bash 106 ./teaparty.sh 107 ``` 108 109 Open http://localhost:8081 in your browser. The dashboard shows active sessions, dispatches, and CfA state transitions in real time. 110 111 ### Run a session (CLI) 112 113 ```bash 114 # Full CfA lifecycle: intent -> planning -> execution 115 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator "Build a feature that does X" 116 117 # Phase control 118 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --intent-only "Explore this idea" 119 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --plan-only "Plan this feature" 120 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --execute-only "Just build it" 121 122 # Inject pre-written artifacts 123 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --intent-file INTENT.md "Build on this" 124 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --plan-file PLAN.md "Execute this plan" 125 126 # Fully autonomous (no human prompts) 127 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --no-human --execute-only "Automated task" 128 129 # Resume a crashed or interrupted session 130 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --resume SESSION_ID 131 132 # Diagnostics 133 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --dry-run -p myproject "Show memory + proxy state" 134 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator -v "Verbose: trace proxy decisions and artifacts" 135 uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --flat "Disable hierarchical dispatch" 136 ``` 137 138 ### Run tests 139 140 ```bash 141 uv run pytest projects/POC/orchestrator/tests/ --tb=short -q 142 ``` 143 144 ### Build the docs 145 146 ```bash 147 uv run mkdocs serve # local preview at http://localhost:8000 148 uv run mkdocs build # build to site/ 149 ``` 150 151 ## Documentation 152 153 Full design documentation is published at **[dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/)**. 154 155 | Section | Contents | 156 |---------|----------| 157 | [Conceptual Design](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/) | Architecture, CfA protocol, intent engineering, strategic planning, learning system, hierarchical teams, human proxies | 158 | [Detailed Design](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/detailed-design/) | Agent runtime, CfA state machine implementation, approval gate, learning system internals | 159 | [Experimental Results](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/experimental-results/) | Ablative experiments across all four pillars: CfA backtracks, hierarchical vs. flat, proxy convergence, scoped retrieval, cost-quality frontier | 160 161 ## License 162 163 All rights reserved. 164

file.content:
TeaParty

A research platform for durable, scalable agent coordination

> **Status: Experimental.** TeaParty is an active research project exploring how humans and AI agents can work together on complex tasks. The ideas are promising, the architecture is evolving, and things break regularly. Nothing here is production-ready. If you're interested in the research direction, welcome — but expect rough edges, incomplete features, and frequent redesigns.

Documentation · Experimental Results · Issues · Contributing

--- Current agent systems fail at four things: they treat requests as complete specifications (**intent gap**), they offer only a binary dial between full autonomy and constant oversight (**escalation failure**), they cannot recover when execution reveals that the plan — or the intent itself — was wrong (**no backtracking**), and they lose coherence as context grows (**context rot**). TeaParty is building toward mixed teams where humans and AI agents collaborate on increasingly complex projects — not agents as tools, and not agents as autonomous replacements, but genuine teams where each member contributes what they do best.

The POC dashboard: projects, sessions with CfA state tracking, and task prompts at a glance.

## Four Research Pillars ### Conversation for Action *Addresses: intent gap, backtracking* A three-phase protocol — **Intent**, **Planning**, **Execution** — formalized as a state machine with explicit transitions and ten cross-phase backtracks. Each phase produces artifacts that make implicit context explicit. Approval gates between phases are learning opportunities where the system observes human corrections and preferences. The key insight is backtracking. When execution reveals a flawed plan, the system returns to planning. When planning reveals a flawed intent, the system returns to intent. When final review reveals that the work faithfully implements an intent that turns out to be wrong, the system backtracks all the way from the end to the beginning. This is the most expensive transition — and without it, the only option is to ship work that misses the point.

The CfA state machine: three phases with synthesis loops and ten cross-phase backtrack transitions. Full specification →

### Hierarchical Teams *Addresses: context rot, scoping* Complex work requires complex team structures. A single flat team trying to coordinate strategy and write every file hits context limits and loses coherence. TeaParty separates strategic coordination from tactical execution: an uber team manages strategy while subteams execute in parallel, each in its own process with its own context window. Liaison agents bridge levels, compressing context at each boundary. The uber lead never sees raw file content; subteam workers never see cross-team coordination. ``` Project Lead / | \ Coding Writing Research <- liaison agents (context boundary) Liaison Liaison Liaison | | | [team] [team] [team] <- isolated subteams (separate processes) ``` [Hierarchical Teams →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/hierarchical-teams/) ### Human Proxy Agents *Addresses: escalation failure* As agent teams grow, the human becomes a bottleneck. The proxy agent learns to stand in for the human — answering clarifying questions, responding to escalations, approving plans — based on an evolving model of what the human would decide. Asymmetric regret weighting ensures false approvals cost more than false escalations. The proxy earns autonomy through demonstrated alignment, not configuration. The proxy's governing principle: **understand first, act second.** Before agents produce artifacts, the proxy runs an intake dialog — formulating predictions about what the human wants and checking them. Where predictions are confident, it answers for the human. Where they're uncertain, it escalates. The delta between predictions and actual answers is the highest-value learning signal in the system. [Human Proxy Agents →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/human-proxies/) ### Hierarchical Memory and Learning *Addresses: context rot, scoping* Learning is a retrieval problem — getting the right knowledge to the right agent at the right moment. Three learning purposes (organizational norms, task procedures, proxy preferences) feed a promotion chain that moves validated learnings up through the hierarchy. Four learning moments (prospective, in-flight, corrective, retrospective) capture knowledge at the points where it matters most. [Learning System →](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/learning-system/) ## Bootstrapping TeaParty eats its own dogfood. The platform's documentation, design artifacts, and implementation are produced by hierarchical agent teams running the TeaParty POC — the same coordination patterns we're researching are the ones we use to build. Every page in this documentation, every architectural decision, and every line of code has been produced or reviewed by agent teams operating under the protocols described above. ## Quick Start ### Prerequisites - Python 3.11+ - [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) package manager - [Claude Code CLI](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) with a valid API key ### Install ```bash git clone https://github.com/dlewissandy/teaparty.git cd teaparty uv sync ``` ### Run the HTML dashboard ```bash ./teaparty.sh ``` Open http://localhost:8081 in your browser. The dashboard shows active sessions, dispatches, and CfA state transitions in real time. ### Run a session (CLI) ```bash # Full CfA lifecycle: intent -> planning -> execution uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator "Build a feature that does X" # Phase control uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --intent-only "Explore this idea" uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --plan-only "Plan this feature" uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --execute-only "Just build it" # Inject pre-written artifacts uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --intent-file INTENT.md "Build on this" uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --plan-file PLAN.md "Execute this plan" # Fully autonomous (no human prompts) uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --no-human --execute-only "Automated task" # Resume a crashed or interrupted session uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --resume SESSION_ID # Diagnostics uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --dry-run -p myproject "Show memory + proxy state" uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator -v "Verbose: trace proxy decisions and artifacts" uv run python -m projects.POC.orchestrator --flat "Disable hierarchical dispatch" ``` ### Run tests ```bash uv run pytest projects/POC/orchestrator/tests/ --tb=short -q ``` ### Build the docs ```bash uv run mkdocs serve # local preview at http://localhost:8000 uv run mkdocs build # build to site/ ``` ## Documentation Full design documentation is published at **[dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/)**. | Section | Contents | |---------|----------| | [Conceptual Design](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/) | Architecture, CfA protocol, intent engineering, strategic planning, learning system, hierarchical teams, human proxies | | [Detailed Design](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/detailed-design/) | Agent runtime, CfA state machine implementation, approval gate, learning system internals | | [Experimental Results](https://dlewissandy.github.io/teaparty/experimental-results/) | Ablative experiments across all four pillars: CfA backtracks, hierarchical vs. flat, proxy convergence, scoped retrieval, cost-quality frontier | ## License All rights reserved.

content.thinking:
Now I have a good picture of what TeaParty is. The README already has a Quick Start section, but the user wants a "quick start guide for new users." I need to ask some clarifying questions before writing INTENT.md, since this is a cold start.

Key questions I have:
1. Where does this guide live? Is it a standalone document (QUICKSTART.md), a new page in the docs site (MkDocs), or a replacement/expansion of the existing Quick Start section in README.md?
2. Who is the target audience — developers who want to contribute/build on TeaParty, or researchers exploring the concepts?


3. What scope — just installation and first run, or a full CfA session walkthrough?
4. Does this supplement the existing Quick Start or replace it?

The current README Quick Start is pretty terse — it covers prerequisites, install, running the dashboard, running a session, running tests, and building docs. But a "guide for new users" suggests something more educational that explains the concepts and what's happening, not just the commands to run. I should ask the user to clarify what they're looking for before I draft anything.

content.text:
I've read through the README and docs structure. The project already has a Quick Start section in README.md — terse install/run commands with no explanation of what's actually happening. A guide for new users could go a lot of different directions depending on what you mean by "new user."

Let me check my understanding before I write the intent.

content.thinking: Let me ask a focused question about the quick start guide.

input.question:
The README already has a Quick Start section — install commands, how to run the dashboard, how to run a CLI session. So I want to make sure I understand what you're after before I write the intent.

A few things I'm uncertain about:

1. **Where does this live?** A new standalone doc page in the MkDocs site (docs/), a QUICKSTART.md at the repo root, or an expansion of the existing README section?

2. **Who is the target reader?** Someone who wants to try the system (run a session, see the dashboard) vs. someone who wants to understand the research concepts well enough to contribute or extend?

3. **What's the gap you're filling?** The current Quick Start gives commands but no explanation of what's happening — is the goal to add that narrative ("here's what the CfA protocol is doing when you run this command"), or is it more focused on getting someone to a working first session without confusion?
